section.title: The Incomplete Conquest
section.sectionRationale: The book of Judges opens where Joshua ended, but the tone is already darkening. Israel asks the right question, who will go up first, and Judah wins real victories under the LORD's hand. Yet verse by verse the refrain shifts from the LORD delivered to they could not, and then to they did not drive out. Tribe after tribe leaves the Canaanites in the land, and the chapter ends not in triumph but in a lengthening catalogue of compromise that will poison the whole era.
section.thesis: So long as Israel trusts the LORD who fights for them, the land is given; but where they settle for coexistence with the Canaanites and their gods, the unfinished conquest becomes the seedbed of the idolatry, oppression, and chaos that fill the rest of the book.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Judah goes up first and the LORD gives victory; Judah and Caleb's further successes shading into the first could not; the mixed accounts of Benjamin and Joseph; and the descending litany of tribes who did not drive out the Canaanites.

units.label: Judah goes up first
units.note: the LORD delivers the land into their hands

verses.text: After the death of Joshua, the Israelites inquired of the LORD, “Who will be the first to go up and fight for us against the Canaanites?”
“Judah shall go up,” answered the LORD. “Indeed, I have {{nathan|delivered}} the land into their hands.”

Then the men of Judah said to their brothers the Simeonites, “Come up with us to our allotted territory, and let us fight against the Canaanites. And we likewise will go with you to your territory.” So the Simeonites went with them.
When Judah attacked, the LORD delivered the Canaanites and Perizzites into their hands, and they struck down ten thousand men at Bezek.
And there they found Adoni-bezek and fought against him, striking down the Canaanites and Perizzites.
As Adoni-bezek fled, they pursued him, seized him, and cut off his thumbs and big toes.

Then Adoni-bezek said, “Seventy kings with their thumbs and big toes cut off have gathered the scraps under my table. As I have done to them, so God has repaid me.” And they brought him to Jerusalem, where he died.

units.label: Judah and Caleb press on
units.note: the first could not drive out

verses.text: Then the men of Judah fought against Jerusalem and captured it. They put the city to the sword and set it on fire.
Afterward, the men of Judah marched down to fight against the Canaanites living in the hill country, in the Negev, and in the foothills.
Judah also marched against the Canaanites who were living in Hebron (formerly known as Kiriath-arba), and they struck down Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai.
From there they marched against the inhabitants of Debir (formerly known as Kiriath-sepher).
And Caleb said, “To the man who strikes down Kiriath-sepher and captures it, I will give my daughter Acsah in marriage.”
So Othniel son of Caleb’s younger brother Kenaz captured the city, and Caleb gave his daughter Acsah to him in marriage.
One day Acsah came to Othniel and urged him to ask her father for a field. When she got off her donkey, Caleb asked her, “What do you desire?”
“Give me a blessing,” she answered. “Since you have given me land in the Negev, give me springs of water as well.” So Caleb gave her both the upper and lower springs.
Now the descendants of Moses’ father-in-law, the Kenite, went up with the men of Judah from the City of Palms to the Wilderness of Judah in the Negev near Arad. They went to live among the people.
Then the men of Judah went with their brothers the Simeonites, attacked the Canaanites living in Zephath, and devoted the city to {{cherem|destruction}}. So it was called Hormah.
And Judah also captured Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron, each with its territory.
The LORD was with Judah, and they took possession of the hill country; but they could not {{yarash|drive out}} the inhabitants of the plains because they had chariots of iron.
Just as Moses had promised, Judah gave Hebron to Caleb, who drove out the descendants of the three sons of Anak.
The Benjamites, however, failed to {{yarash|drive out}} the Jebusites living in Jerusalem. So to this day the Jebusites live there among the Benjamites.
The house of Joseph also attacked Bethel, and the LORD was with them.
They sent spies to Bethel (formerly known as Luz),
and when the spies saw a man coming out of the city, they said to him, “Please show us how to get into the city, and we will treat you kindly.”
So the man showed them the entrance to the city, and they put the city to the sword but released that man and all his family.
And the man went to the land of the Hittites, built a city, and called it Luz, which is its name to this day.
At that time Manasseh failed to {{yarash|drive out}} the inhabitants of Beth-shean, Taanach, Dor, Ibleam, Megiddo, and their villages; for the Canaanites were determined to dwell in that land.
When Israel became stronger, they pressed the Canaanites into forced labor, but they never drove them out completely.
Ephraim also failed to drive out the Canaanites living in Gezer; so the Canaanites continued to dwell among them in Gezer.
Zebulun failed to drive out the inhabitants of Kitron and Nahalol; so the Canaanites lived among them and served as forced laborers.
Asher failed to drive out the inhabitants of Acco, Sidon, Ahlab, Achzib, Helbah, Aphik, and Rehob.
So the Asherites lived among the Canaanite inhabitants of the land, because they did not drive them out.

Naphtali failed to drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath. So the Naphtalites also lived among the Canaanite inhabitants of the land, but the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath served them as forced laborers.
The Amorites forced the Danites into the hill country and did not allow them to come down into the plain.
And the Amorites were determined to dwell in Mount Heres, Aijalon, and Shaalbim. But when the house of Joseph grew in strength, they pressed the Amorites into forced labor.
And the border of the Amorites extended from the Ascent of Akrabbim to Sela and beyond.

nathan.gloss: To give, hand over, deliver. In verse 2 the LORD says He has already given the land into Judah's hands; the victory is a gift received by faith, not a prize won by force. The whole chapter measures Israel by whether they take hold of what God has already given.

cherem.gloss: The devotion of a city or its people wholly to the LORD, often by destruction, as an act of judgment on entrenched evil. The BSB footnote explains that forms of cherem refer to giving persons or things over to God. Zephath is placed under the ban and renamed Hormah, destruction; the tragedy of the chapter is that this thoroughness is the exception, not the rule.

yarash.gloss: To dispossess, drive out, take possession of an inheritance. This is the key verb of the chapter and its refrain of failure: could not drive out, failed to drive out, did not drive them out. To leave the Canaanites in the land was to leave their gods, and the unfinished dispossession becomes the snare that entangles the whole book.

basic.context: Judges chapter 1 picks up right after the death of Joshua. The land had been broadly conquered, but pockets of Canaanites remained, and now each tribe must finish taking its own inheritance. Israel begins well: they ask the LORD who should go first, He names Judah, and Judah wins real victories. But the chapter slowly turns. First Judah could not drive out the people of the plains; then Benjamin failed at Jerusalem; then tribe after tribe, Manasseh, Ephraim, Zebulun, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan, did not drive out the Canaanites but settled down beside them. The big question is: will Israel trust God enough to finish what He has given them, or will they make peace with the very evil they were sent to remove?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that describes a downward drift, not a manual commanding holy war today. We are meant to feel the momentum: the same phrase repeated with mounting sadness, from the LORD delivered to they could not to they did not. The warfare belongs to a specific moment in redemptive history, God's judgment on the Canaanites and the securing of the land of promise. What carries across to us is the danger of half-obedience, of tolerating the very thing God told us to put to death.
basic.originalLanguages: In verse 2 the LORD says He has already nathan, given, the land into Judah's hands; the conquest is a gift to be received. At Zephath the city is placed under cherem, devoted wholly to the LORD, and renamed Hormah. But the verb that stamps the chapter is yarash, to dispossess or drive out. It rings out again and again in the negative: could not drive out, failed to drive out, did not drive them out. Leaving the people meant leaving their gods, and the unfinished driving out sets the trap for everything that follows.
basic.typology: Judah goes up first, and the LORD is with him, faintly foreshadowing the Lion of the tribe of Judah who leads His people to a victory that will not fail. Caleb and Othniel, who take hold of their inheritance in full faith, stand as bright pictures of the obedient rest that Christ finally secures. And the failure of Israel to drive out its enemies points ahead to our need of a greater Joshua, Jesus, who alone conquers sin fully and gives His people an inheritance that cannot be lost.
basic.christ: Israel could not finish the conquest; every tribe left an enemy in the land, and that unfinished work doomed the era. This exposes our need for a Savior who does not leave the work half done. Where Israel tolerated the Canaanite, Jesus went to the cross to deal with sin at the root, and where the tribes could not drive out their foes, Christ has disarmed the powers and will finally banish all evil from His creation. He is the true and better Judah who goes up first for His people and wins.
basic.god: God is faithful and generous: He answers when His people ask, He names the tribe, He goes with them, and He delivers real victories. He is also holy: the Canaanite cities are judged for entrenched wickedness, as Adoni-bezek himself admits, so God has repaid me. And God is patient, giving Israel every advantage; the failure that follows lies with the people who would not trust and obey, not with any lack in Him.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 23 warned that if Israel clung to the remnant of these nations they would become a snare. Deuteronomy 7 commanded them to drive out the nations and make no covenant with them. Genesis 49 promises that the scepter will not depart from Judah. And Hebrews 4 speaks of a greater rest that Joshua could not give, secured in Christ.
basic.oneStory: After Joshua, Israel is meant to complete the conquest and enjoy the inheritance God has given. Instead the chapter records a slow slide into compromise, tribe by tribe leaving the Canaanites and their gods in the land. That unfinished work is the seedbed of the whole tragic book, and it cries out for a greater Joshua, Jesus of the tribe of Judah, who wins the victory His people could not and secures an inheritance that will never be lost.

context.literary: Chapter 1 is the first half of a two-part introduction to Judges, chapters 1 to 2 setting the stage before the cycle of individual judges begins. It is deliberately structured as a descent: it opens with united inquiry of the LORD and Judah's God-given victories, then introduces the first note of failure at verse 19, could not drive out, and closes with a rapid-fire list of six tribes who did not drive out the Canaanites. The repeated verb drive out and the growing geographical scope of failure trace a downward staircase.
context.historical: The setting is the land of Canaan in the generation after Joshua, before any central king or capital. Israel is a loose federation of tribes, each responsible for securing its own allotted territory. The Canaanite city-states in the fertile plains fielded chariots of iron, a formidable military technology, which is offered as the human reason Judah could not clear the lowlands. Forced labor was a common ancient way of subjugating a conquered people, but it fell short of the driving out God had commanded.
context.canonical: This chapter deliberately echoes and inverts the book of Joshua, where the LORD gave the whole land and the people largely obeyed. Here the same land, the same enemies, and the same promises meet a people whose resolve is failing. It sets up chapter 2, where the Angel of the LORD explains the meaning of the compromise, and the whole book, which shows what happens when the conquest is left unfinished. The bright glimpses of Judah, Caleb, and Othniel prepare the reader to look for a faithful deliverer from Judah's line.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, at the very outset of the book, how Israel's failure to fully drive out the Canaanites, beginning even where they were strongest, set the stage for the idolatry and disorder that follow, and so to press the reader toward the need for a faithful king and deliverer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes both faith and failure; it does not prescribe the conquest as a pattern for the church, whose warfare is spiritual, not territorial. The abiding lessons are that God gives victory to those who trust and obey Him, that half-hearted obedience leaves a snare in place, and that tolerated sin, like a tolerated enemy, does not stay quiet but grows.

debates.question: Why could Judah not drive out the people of the plains despite the LORD being with them?

views.label: A failure of faith
views.case: The text stresses that the LORD was with Judah, so the chariots of iron were no real obstacle to the God who had drowned Pharaoh's chariots in the sea; the could not reflects a shrinking of faith, and the parallel later verses openly call it a failure to drive out. The problem lay in Israel, not in God's power.

views.label: A note of real difficulty acknowledged
views.case: The narrator honestly records the military reality, that iron chariots dominated open ground, to explain the human circumstances; on this reading could not marks genuine hardship, though it still stands under the greater truth that God had promised the land and could have given it had they pressed on in faith.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter warns against letting visible obstacles eclipse God's clear promise; the tragedy is not that the task was hard but that hardship became an excuse to stop short of full obedience.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a handful of Hebrew words that carry its message. In verse 2 the LORD declares He has nathan, given, the land into Judah's hands: the conquest is framed from the start as a gift already granted, to be laid hold of by faith. At Zephath the city is placed under cherem, devoted wholly to the LORD in judgment, and renamed Hormah, destruction, the one place in the chapter where the ban is carried out in full. But the word that beats through the whole passage is yarash, to dispossess or drive out. It appears in triumph when Caleb drives out the sons of Anak, and then, with mounting sorrow, in the negative: Judah could not drive out, Benjamin failed to drive out, and then Manasseh, Ephraim, Zebulun, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan did not drive them out. Because to drive out the people was also to remove their gods, this failed dispossession is the quiet catastrophe of the chapter, the loose thread from which the whole book unravels.

typology.type: Judah goes up first and the LORD is with him
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, who goes ahead of His people and wins a victory that does not fail, Genesis 49:9-10 and Revelation 5:5.

typology.type: Caleb and Othniel taking full hold of their inheritance
typology.fulfillment: The whole-hearted rest and inheritance that Christ secures for His people, a rest Joshua's generation only partly entered, Hebrews 4:8-11.

typology.type: The unfinished conquest and tolerated enemy
typology.fulfillment: Our need of Jesus, the greater Joshua, who deals with sin at the root and will finally drive every enemy from His creation, Romans 16:20 and 1 Corinthians 15:24-25.

study.christ: The great ache of this chapter is a work left unfinished. Every tribe leaves an enemy in the land, and that half-done conquest becomes the ruin of the era. It exposes what we most need: a Savior who does not stop short. Where Israel could not drive out the Canaanite, and worse, would not, Jesus went to the cross to deal with sin itself, disarming the powers and authorities and triumphing over them. He is the true and better Judah who goes up first for His people, and the greater Joshua who gives an inheritance that cannot be defiled or lost. The faith of Caleb and Othniel, who took hold of all God had promised, is a small picture of the rest Christ has now fully won; and the failure of the tribes drives us to trust not our own resolve but His finished work.
study.god: God shows Himself faithful and free-handed at every turn: He answers Israel's inquiry, appoints Judah, goes with them, and delivers real and named victories. He is also holy and just; the judgment on the Canaanite cities is the ripening of long wickedness, and Adoni-bezek confesses that his own cruelty has come back on his head, so God has repaid me. And God is patient and generous, giving Israel every advantage and every promise. The failure that unfolds is never traced to Him but to a people who let hard obstacles and easy compromise turn them aside from trusting the God who had already given them the land.

crossReferences.note: Joshua warned that if Israel clung to the remnant of these nations, they would become a snare and a trap, the very thing chapter 1 sets in motion.
The command to drive out the nations, make no covenant, and tear down their altars, which Israel here leaves undone.
Jacob's blessing that Judah's brothers will praise him and the scepter will not depart from him, behind Judah going up first.
Joshua did not give the people final rest; a greater rest remains, secured in Christ, beyond the partial inheritance of Judges 1.

study.oneStory: After Joshua's death, Israel is called to complete the conquest and settle fully into the inheritance the LORD has given. Instead this chapter charts a slow, tribe-by-tribe slide into compromise, leaving the Canaanites and their gods in the land. That unfinished work is the seed from which the whole tragic book of Judges will grow, and it makes the reader long for a greater Joshua and a faithful king from Judah, Jesus, who wins the victory His people could not, drives out every enemy, and gives an inheritance that will never be lost.

quiz.q: How does the chapter frame Judah's early victories?

options.text: As a gift, the LORD had already delivered the land into their hands and went with them.
options.note: Verse 2 has the LORD say He has given the land; the victory is received by faith, not achieved alone.

options.text: As the reward of Judah's superior military strength.
options.note: Judah was strong, but the text credits the LORD's delivering hand, not Judah's might, as the reason.

options.text: As victories Judah won without any help.
options.note: This misses that the LORD was explicitly with Judah and delivered the enemy into their hands.

options.text: As battles the LORD had forbidden them to fight.
options.note: The opposite: the LORD Himself appointed Judah to go up first.

quiz.q: What is the repeated refrain that stamps the second half of the chapter?

options.text: That tribe after tribe did not drive out the Canaanites but let them remain in the land.
options.note: From verse 19 onward the phrase failed to or did not drive out recurs six times, tracing the drift.

options.text: That the tribes pressed the Canaanites into forced labor.
options.note: True in several cases, but forced labor is precisely the compromise that fell short of driving them out.

options.text: That the Canaanites were completely destroyed everywhere.
options.note: Only at Hormah was a city fully devoted; the chapter's point is how much was left undone.

options.text: That the tribes returned the land to the Canaanites willingly.
options.note: They did not hand it back; they simply failed to complete the conquest and settled beside them.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter matter for the rest of the book of Judges?

options.text: The tolerated Canaanites and their gods become the snare that leads Israel into the idolatry and chaos that follow.
options.note: The unfinished conquest is the seedbed of the whole downward cycle, as chapter 2 will explain.

options.text: It shows that Israel's borders were finally secure.
options.note: There were real gains, but the emphasis falls on what was left undone, not on security achieved.

options.text: It simply lists tribal territories with no larger point.
options.note: It is far more than a map; it is a deliberate account of spiritual drift.

options.text: It proves the Canaanites were never really a threat.
options.note: They became a lasting snare and thorn, exactly the threat God had warned of.

quiz.q: How does Judah going up first point us to Christ?

options.text: Judah leads the way in victory under God, foreshadowing the Lion of Judah, Jesus, who goes ahead and wins a victory that does not fail.
options.note: Genesis 49 and Revelation 5 tie Judah's leadership to Christ, the true and better Judah.

options.text: Judah was simply the largest and most capable tribe.
options.note: Judah did lead, but the deeper significance is the promised line and rescuer from Judah.

options.text: It teaches that whoever fights hardest earns God's favor.
options.note: The victory was God's gift, not a wage earned by effort, and points to Christ, not self-effort.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: Scripture explicitly ties Judah's leadership to the Messiah who comes from that tribe.

quiz.q: What does Adoni-bezek's confession, so God has repaid me, reveal?

options.text: That God is just, repaying entrenched cruelty even by the mouth of a pagan king.
options.note: His words acknowledge a moral order under God's justice behind the conquest.

options.text: That the Israelites were needlessly cruel to their enemies.
options.note: The maiming is grim, but the verse frames it as measured justice on a man who had done the same to seventy kings.

options.text: That Adoni-bezek was innocent and wrongly treated.
options.note: By his own admission he had mutilated seventy kings; he is not portrayed as innocent.

options.text: That there is no justice in what happened, only random violence.
options.note: The king himself names it as repayment, pointing to God's justice, not randomness.

questions: Israel began by asking the LORD who should go up first. Where do you need to start by inquiring of God rather than rushing ahead in your own plans?
The chapter slides from the LORD delivered to they could not to they did not. Where has an obstacle in your life quietly turned from a difficulty into an excuse for half-obedience?
The tribes let the Canaanites remain and pressed them into forced labor instead of driving them out. What sin have you learned to manage and live beside rather than put to death?
Caleb and Othniel took full hold of the inheritance God promised. What promise of God are you being invited to lay hold of in faith right now?
The unfinished conquest cries out for a greater Joshua. How does resting in Christ's finished victory free you to fight sin from a place of security rather than fear?

translationNotes.note: Devoted the city to destruction renders the Hebrew cherem; the BSB footnote explains that forms of cherem refer to giving persons or things wholly over to the LORD, by destruction or as an offering. Hormah means destruction.

The BSB reads that Judah captured Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron, while a footnote records that the Septuagint reads Judah did not capture these coastal cities, which fits the chapter's theme of incomplete conquest.
Could not drive out translates the key verb yarash, to dispossess; the same verb recurs through the chapter, and the honest could not stands in tension with the LORD was with Judah in the same verse.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 1:1-36. An em dash in verse 18 (Ekron, each with its territory) was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes present.
